questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Scarcity + Choice · $100: Next-best alternative given up when a choice is made.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative sacrificed when any economic choice is made — it exists even when no money changes hands. On a PPF, the opportunity cost of producing more guns is the butter given up; in comparative advantage analysis, the nation with the lower opportunity cost should specialize. AP FRQ trap: opportunity cost is not the sum of all alternatives, only the single best one foregone.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Scarcity + Choice · $200: Comparing additional benefits and additional costs.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Marginal analysis is the decision rule of comparing the additional benefit (MB) of one more unit against its additional cost (MC): act if MB >= MC, stop when MB < MC. This drives profit maximization (MR = MC), consumer equilibrium (MU/P equal across goods), and optimal pollution abatement. It is the foundational logic behind every AP Micro and Macro optimization question.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Scarcity + Choice · $300: Ability to produce at lower opportunity cost.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage exists when a producer can make a good at a lower opportunity cost than a trading partner — it is the basis for all mutually beneficial trade. Even if one country has absolute advantage in everything, both gain by specializing in their CA good and trading. On the PPF, the slope reveals opportunity cost; the flatter slope for a good signals CA in that good. Terms of trade must lie between the two domestic opportunity costs for both parties to benefit.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Scarcity + Choice · $400: Principle that producing more of one good usually requires giving up increasing amounts of another.
questions.explanation: The law of increasing opportunity cost states that as production of one good rises, the opportunity cost of each additional unit grows because resources are not equally suited to all uses. On the PPF this produces the characteristic bowed-out (concave) shape: the slope steepens as you move along the curve. If a PPF is straight (constant opportunity cost), resources are perfectly substitutable — a testable distinction on AP FRQs.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Scarcity + Choice · $500: Model showing scarcity, opportunity cost, efficiency, and trade-offs.
questions.explanation: The PPF maps all efficient combinations of two goods given fixed resources and technology. Points on the curve are productively efficient; points inside indicate unemployment or inefficiency; points outside are unattainable. Outward shifts signal economic growth (more resources or better technology). The slope at any point equals the opportunity cost. AP FRQs frequently ask students to illustrate growth, trade, or comparative advantage using this graph.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Models · $100: Condition where wants exceed available resources.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Scarcity is the condition where human wants exceed available resources, making economics necessary. Because resources (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship) are limited, every choice involves an opportunity cost. Scarcity forces society to answer the three economic questions: what to produce, how to produce, and for whom. It applies to every economy regardless of type.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Models · $200: Focusing production on a narrower task or good.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Specialization means focusing production on goods in which a producer has comparative advantage, which raises productivity and enables gains from trade. Adam Smith's pin factory showed that dividing labor into specialized steps multiplies output. In international trade, nations specialize in CA goods and trade for others, pushing consumption beyond the domestic PPF and increasing living standards for both partners.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Models · $300: Rewards or penalties that shape behavior.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Incentives are rewards or penalties that influence economic behavior — prices, profits, wages, taxes, and subsidies all function as incentives. Rising prices signal producers to expand supply and buyers to seek substitutes. Poorly designed incentives create unintended consequences (e.g., a price ceiling removes the incentive to build housing). AP FRQs routinely ask how a policy change alters producer or consumer incentives.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Models · $400: Using resources to maximize total benefit.
questions.explanation: Economic efficiency means resources generate maximum total benefit at minimum cost. Productive efficiency occurs at minimum ATC; allocative efficiency occurs where P = MC, directing goods to their highest-valued uses. Both types prevail in long-run perfect competition. Monopoly violates allocative efficiency because it produces where MR = MC but charges P > MC, creating deadweight loss.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Models · $500: Additional benefit gained from one more unit of an action or good.
questions.explanation: The marginal benefit (MB) curve shows the additional satisfaction from each successive unit — it slopes downward due to diminishing marginal utility. In markets, the demand curve is each consumer's MB curve. Optimal output occurs where MB = MC; consumer surplus is the area below MB and above the price. On social-cost graphs, marginal social benefit (MSB) lies above private MB by the amount of the external benefit per unit.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Comparative Advantage · $100: An economy where government makes most economic decisions.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: A command economy is one where a central authority makes the fundamental decisions about what, how, and for whom to produce, replacing the price mechanism with planning directives. Command economies can coordinate rapid industrialization but suffer from information problems, lack of consumer signals, and reduced innovation compared with market systems.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Comparative Advantage · $200: Economy combining markets and government intervention.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: A mixed economy combines private markets with government intervention to address market failures, provide public goods, redistribute income, and regulate externalities. Virtually all modern economies are mixed: markets set most prices and quantities while government provides defense, enforces property rights, and operates safety-net programs. The blend of market and government determines economic outcomes.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Comparative Advantage · $300: Legal protections over ownership and use of resources, goods, or ideas.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Property rights are legal protections allowing individuals and firms to own, use, and transfer resources and products. Secure property rights create incentives to invest, innovate, and maintain assets, forming the institutional foundation for markets. Without enforceable property rights — as in a tragedy-of-the-commons scenario — resources tend to be overused. Patents and copyrights extend property rights to intellectual goods.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Comparative Advantage · $400: Rules and organizations, such as property rights, courts, banks, and firms, that structure economic activity.
questions.explanation: Economic institutions are the rules, norms, and organizations — property rights, contract law, banking systems, regulatory agencies, courts — that structure economic interactions. Strong institutions reduce transaction costs, enforce agreements, and create the stable environment needed for investment and growth. Cross-country income differences are substantially explained by institutional quality.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Comparative Advantage · $500: Greater output with the same resources, distinct from lower opportunity cost.
questions.explanation: Absolute advantage means producing a good using fewer resources than a competitor — it measures raw productivity, not opportunity cost. A country can have absolute advantage in everything yet still gain from trade based on comparative advantage. AP FRQs often give a production table and require students to correctly distinguish which party has absolute advantage versus comparative advantage — confusing the two is the most common error.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Demand + Supply · $100: Model showing money, goods, services, and resources moving between households and firms.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: The circular flow model shows households and firms interacting in two markets: households supply factors (labor, capital, land) in factor markets and receive income; they spend income on goods in product markets, generating firm revenue. Real flows of goods and resources move opposite to money flows. An expanded version includes government and the foreign sector to capture the full macroeconomic picture tested on AP FRQs.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Demand + Supply · $200: Frontier model showing trade-offs and efficient combinations under resource limits.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: The consumption possibilities frontier (CPF) shows all combinations of goods a country can consume given its resources and trade opportunities. Without trade, the CPF coincides with the PPF; with trade, specialization and exchange allow the CPF to extend beyond the PPF, demonstrating that trade based on comparative advantage expands consumption possibilities beyond domestic production limits.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Demand + Supply · $300: The economic system where prices and private choices guide allocation.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: A market economy decentralizes resource allocation through the price mechanism: prices transmit information about scarcity, coordinate millions of independent decisions, and provide profit incentives for innovation and efficiency — Adam Smith's invisible hand. Markets perform well when property rights are secure, information is symmetric, externalities are absent, and competition prevails; failures in these conditions motivate government intervention.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Demand + Supply · $400: Additional cost created by producing or choosing one more unit.
questions.explanation: The marginal cost (MC) curve shows the additional cost of producing one more unit; it slopes upward due to diminishing marginal returns. In competitive markets, MC above AVC is the firm's short-run supply curve (firms supply where P = MC). Allocative efficiency requires P = MC. On social-cost diagrams, marginal social cost (MSC) lies above private MC by the amount of the external cost per unit produced.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Demand + Supply · $500: Prices, profits, losses, rewards, or penalties that push buyers and sellers to change behavior.
questions.explanation: Market incentives are profit motives, price signals, and competitive pressures that guide resource allocation without central direction. Rising prices signal producers to expand supply and attract new entrants; falling prices signal the reverse. Profits pull resources toward valued activities; losses push them away. AP FRQs test how policy changes (taxes, price ceilings, subsidies) alter these incentive signals and change equilibrium outcomes.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Equilibrium · $100: Trade in which both sides choose to participate because each expects to gain.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Voluntary exchange occurs when both buyer and seller freely agree because each expects to gain — the buyer values the good more than the price paid (consumer surplus), and the seller values the price more than cost (producer surplus). Total surplus equals the sum of both, maximized at competitive equilibrium. Voluntary exchange is the mechanism that makes competitive markets welfare-maximizing, a key AP Micro efficiency result.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Equilibrium · $200: Ratio comparing export prices to import prices, showing how much imports a country can buy with its exports.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Terms of trade (TOT) measure the rate at which one good or currency exchanges for another in international trade, often expressed as the price of exports relative to imports. Both trading parties gain when TOT lies strictly between their domestic opportunity costs. A favorable shift (export prices rising relative to import prices) improves a nation's real income; an adverse shift worsens it — a key AP FRQ topic.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Equilibrium · $300: Choices that require giving up one option, benefit, or goal to get another.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Trade-offs arise from scarcity: choosing more of one good or goal requires accepting less of another. On the PPF, every point represents a trade-off between two goods; moving along the curve converts units of one into units of the other at the rate of opportunity cost. Society faces macro trade-offs too — e.g., the short-run inflation-unemployment trade-off shown on the Phillips Curve, or the efficiency-equity trade-off in redistribution policy.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Equilibrium · $400: Trade-off between maximizing total output and distributing benefits more fairly.
questions.explanation: The efficiency-equity trade-off captures the tension between maximizing total output (efficiency) and distributing it fairly (equity). Policies that raise equity — progressive taxation, minimum wages, transfer payments — may reduce efficiency by distorting incentives; purely efficient markets may produce very unequal distributions. AP Economics treats equity as a normative goal (value judgment) while efficiency is measured positively using total surplus and deadweight loss.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Equilibrium · $500: Choosing the option where expected marginal benefits are greater than expected marginal costs.
questions.explanation: Rational decision-making assumes individuals weigh expected marginal benefits against marginal costs and choose the option that maximizes net benefit. This underpins supply, demand, consumer theory (MU/P rule), and firm behavior (MR = MC). Rationality does not require perfect information — just consistent self-interest. Behavioral economics documents systematic cognitive biases that challenge pure rationality, but AP Micro and Macro still operate from the rational actor baseline.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Foundations of Economics · Economic Reasoning · $700: This simplified representation helps economists analyze choices, trade-offs, and cause-effect relationships.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: An economic model is a simplified representation — graphical, mathematical, or verbal — that isolates key relationships to generate testable predictions. Models like the PPF, AD-AS, supply-and-demand, and circular flow abstract away complexity to reveal core mechanisms. AP Economics tests students' ability to use these models to analyze policy changes, market outcomes, and trade, judging models by their predictive power rather than realism.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Elasticity · $100: Responsiveness of quantity demanded to price changes.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Price elasticity of demand (PED) = |%Delta Qd / %Delta P|. Elastic demand (PED > 1) means quantity responds strongly to price; inelastic (PED < 1) means weakly. When demand is elastic, a price cut raises total revenue; when inelastic, a price hike raises total revenue. Determinants include availability of substitutes, necessity vs luxury, time horizon, and share of budget. AP FRQs use PED to determine tax incidence and deadweight loss size.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Elasticity · $200: Legal maximum price that creates a shortage when set below equilibrium.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: A binding price ceiling is set below the equilibrium price, making it the legal maximum. It causes quantity demanded to exceed quantity supplied, creating a persistent shortage. Nonprice rationing (queues, black markets) emerges. On an S-D graph, draw a horizontal line below equilibrium: Qs shrinks left, Qd expands right, and the gap equals the shortage. Consumer surplus partially transfers from some consumers to others, and deadweight loss appears.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Elasticity · $300: Legal minimum price that creates a surplus when set above equilibrium.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: A binding price floor is set above the equilibrium price, causing quantity supplied to exceed quantity demanded and creating a persistent surplus. The minimum wage is the classic example — set above market wage, it creates unemployment (labor surplus). On an S-D graph, draw a horizontal line above equilibrium: Qs rises right, Qd shrinks left, and the gap equals the surplus. Deadweight loss and a reduction in total surplus occur.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Elasticity · $400: Price and quantity where quantity supplied equals quantity demanded.
questions.explanation: Market equilibrium is the price-quantity combination where quantity supplied equals quantity demanded (Qs = Qd), leaving no tendency to change. At prices above equilibrium, surpluses drive price down; below equilibrium, shortages drive price up. Equilibrium changes when supply or demand shifts — a new intersection. AP FRQs test whether students correctly predict both price and quantity changes when two shifts occur simultaneously.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Elasticity · $500: Legal minimum set above equilibrium that can create surplus.
questions.explanation: A price floor is a government-set minimum price; it is effective (binding) only when set above equilibrium. Agricultural price supports are the classic macroeconomic example. A binding floor raises price above equilibrium, lowering Qd, raising Qs, and creating a surplus. Total surplus falls (deadweight loss) because fewer mutually beneficial trades occur. AP FRQs ask students to identify who gains and who loses from a price floor.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Surplus · $100: Quantities consumers are willing and able to buy at each price.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Demand is the entire relationship between price and quantity demanded, represented as a downward-sloping curve (law of demand). A movement along the demand curve is a change in quantity demanded caused by a price change; a shift of the curve reflects a change in a non-price determinant (income, tastes, prices of related goods, expectations, number of buyers). AP FRQs routinely require identifying which category of change occurred.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Surplus · $200: Quantities producers are willing and able to sell at each price.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Supply is the entire relationship between price and quantity supplied, represented as an upward-sloping curve (law of supply). Movement along the curve = change in quantity supplied (due to price change); shift of curve = change in non-price determinant (input costs, technology, number of sellers, government policy, expectations). AP FRQs test whether students correctly draw, label, and shift supply curves to analyze policy impacts.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Surplus · $300: Buyer benefit from paying less than maximum willingness to pay.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Consumer surplus (CS) is the difference between a buyer's maximum willingness to pay and the actual price paid — represented graphically as the area below the demand curve and above the price. CS rises when price falls or demand increases. A tax, price floor, or monopoly pricing reduces CS. Total surplus = CS + PS; competitive equilibrium maximizes total surplus. AP FRQs often ask students to shade and compare CS before and after a policy change.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Surplus · $400: Benefit producers receive from selling above minimum acceptable price.
questions.explanation: Producer surplus (PS) is the difference between the price a seller receives and the minimum price they would accept (opportunity cost) — graphically the area above the supply curve and below the price. PS rises when price rises or supply costs fall. Taxes and price ceilings reduce PS. Together, CS + PS = total surplus, maximized at competitive equilibrium. AP FRQs frequently ask students to identify changes in PS after market interventions.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Surplus · $500: Distribution of a tax burden between buyers and sellers.
questions.explanation: Tax incidence describes who actually bears the burden of a tax regardless of who legally pays it. The burden falls more heavily on whichever side of the market is less elastic: inelastic buyers bear more of a tax; inelastic sellers bear more. Graphically, an excise tax creates a wedge between supply and demand; buyer price rises and seller price falls by amounts inversely proportional to elasticities. AP FRQs often ask students to identify incidence using elasticity comparisons.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Taxes · $100: Goods used together, so a higher price for one lowers demand for the other.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Complementary goods are used together (e.g., cars and gasoline, coffee and cream), so a price increase in one reduces demand for the other (negative cross-price elasticity). If the price of gasoline rises, demand for large cars shifts left. On an S-D diagram, an increase in price of good A shifts the demand curve for complement B leftward, lowering B's equilibrium price and quantity. AP FRQs test whether students correctly predict the direction of the demand shift.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Taxes · $200: Per-unit tax that raises costs, reduces quantity, and splits burden between buyers and sellers.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: An excise tax is a per-unit tax on a specific good that shifts the supply curve upward (or leftward) by the tax amount, raising the buyer's price, lowering the seller's net price, reducing equilibrium quantity, and creating deadweight loss. Tax revenue = tax amount x Qtaxed. Burden is split according to elasticities: the more inelastic side bears more. AP FRQs routinely ask students to show these effects on a supply-and-demand graph.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Taxes · $300: Goods for which demand rises when consumer income rises.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Normal goods are goods for which demand increases when consumer income rises (positive income elasticity of demand, YED > 0). Most goods are normal — as income grows, people buy more. Contrast with inferior goods (YED < 0, demand falls with income) and necessities (0 < YED < 1, demand rises less than proportionally). Knowing a good's income elasticity is essential for predicting demand shifts when income changes — a common AP FRQ prompt.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Taxes · $400: Government payment that lowers costs and encourages production or consumption.
questions.explanation: A subsidy is a government payment per unit that lowers producers' costs, shifting the supply curve rightward (or downward by the subsidy amount). Equilibrium price falls, quantity rises, and both consumer and producer surplus increase — but there is still deadweight loss because output is pushed beyond the socially efficient level unless the subsidy corrects a positive externality. AP FRQs ask students to show the shift, identify the welfare effects, and explain the government's fiscal cost.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Taxes · $500: Goods for which demand falls when consumer income rises.
questions.explanation: Inferior goods are goods for which demand falls as consumer income rises (negative income elasticity, YED < 0). Classic examples include generic store brands, bus travel, and certain fast foods — as income grows, consumers switch to preferred substitutes. During recessions, demand for inferior goods rises as incomes fall. AP FRQs test whether students correctly predict the direction of a demand shift when income changes for inferior vs normal goods.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Controls · $100: Movement of the entire demand curve caused by a non-price determinant of demand.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: A demand curve shift (change in demand) moves the entire curve when a non-price determinant changes: income, prices of related goods (substitutes/complements), consumer tastes, number of buyers, or expectations. A rightward shift increases equilibrium price and quantity; a leftward shift decreases both. Distinguish carefully from a change in quantity demanded (movement along the curve in response to a price change) — AP FRQs penalize confusing the two.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Controls · $200: Movement of the entire supply curve caused by a non-price determinant of supply.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: A supply curve shift (change in supply) moves the entire curve when a non-price determinant changes: input costs, technology, number of producers, government taxes/subsidies, expectations, or resource prices. A rightward shift lowers equilibrium price and raises quantity; a leftward shift raises price and lowers quantity. Distinguish from a change in quantity supplied (movement along the curve). AP FRQs require correctly identifying the shifter and drawing the new equilibrium.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Controls · $300: Demand that responds strongly to price changes.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Elastic demand (PED > 1) means a 1% price increase causes more than a 1% decrease in quantity demanded — consumers are highly responsive. Goods with close substitutes, luxury goods, and goods with long adjustment periods tend to be elastic. When demand is elastic, a price increase reduces total revenue (TR = P x Q falls more from quantity loss than price gain). AP FRQs use elasticity to determine which party bears more of a tax burden.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Controls · $400: Demand that responds weakly to price changes.
questions.explanation: Inelastic demand (PED < 1) means a 1% price increase causes less than a 1% decrease in quantity demanded — consumers are relatively unresponsive to price. Necessities, addictive goods, and goods with few substitutes tend to be inelastic. When demand is inelastic, a price increase raises total revenue. Governments typically tax goods with inelastic demand (gasoline, tobacco) to maximize revenue while minimizing the reduction in output.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Controls · $500: Decision-making by buyers as they compare prices, benefits, income limits, and opportunity costs.
questions.explanation: Consumer choice theory models how rational buyers allocate limited budgets to maximize utility. The utility-maximizing rule is MU_A/P_A = MU_B/P_B across all goods: equalize marginal utility per dollar spent. Graphically, consumers choose the bundle on the highest indifference curve tangent to the budget constraint. AP Micro FRQs may test how price changes or income changes shift the optimal bundle and whether income or substitution effects dominate.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Market Shifts · $100: Goods that can replace each other, so a higher price for one raises demand for the other.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Substitute goods are alternatives that satisfy similar needs (e.g., Coke and Pepsi, butter and margarine), so a price increase in one raises demand for the other (positive cross-price elasticity). If the price of Coke rises, demand for Pepsi shifts right, raising Pepsi's price and quantity. AP FRQs test whether students correctly identify substitutes versus complements and predict the direction of demand shifts across markets.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Market Shifts · $200: Legal maximum price.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: A price ceiling is a legally imposed maximum price; it binds only when set below equilibrium. A binding ceiling creates a shortage (Qd > Qs), leads to nonprice rationing, and may generate black markets. Rent control is the textbook example. Total surplus falls because fewer mutually beneficial trades occur (deadweight loss). A non-binding ceiling (set above equilibrium) has no effect on market outcomes.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Market Shifts · $300: Responsiveness of quantity supplied to price changes.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Price elasticity of supply (PES) = %Delta Qs / %Delta P. Elastic supply (PES > 1) means producers respond strongly to price changes; inelastic supply means they respond weakly. Supply is more elastic over longer time horizons as producers can adjust capacity. PES determines tax incidence: when supply is inelastic relative to demand, sellers bear more of a tax. AP FRQs compare PES and PED to assign tax burden.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Market Shifts · $400: Public action, such as taxes, subsidies, regulation, or price controls, that changes market outcomes.
questions.explanation: Government intervention alters market outcomes through taxes, subsidies, price controls, regulations, or quantity restrictions. Justified interventions address market failures (externalities, public goods, information asymmetries); unjustified ones create deadweight loss by moving output away from the competitive equilibrium. AP FRQs analyze the welfare effects — changes in CS, PS, government revenue, and DWL — of specific interventions.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Market Shifts · $500: Measure of how demand for one good responds to another good's price.
questions.explanation: Cross-price elasticity of demand (CPE) = %Delta Qd_A / %Delta P_B. Positive CPE indicates substitutes (higher price of B raises demand for A); negative CPE indicates complements (higher price of B lowers demand for A); near-zero CPE suggests unrelated goods. AP FRQs may give a CPE value and ask students to identify the relationship between goods and predict demand curve shifts when one good's price changes.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Micro Markets · Market Efficiency · $700: This lost surplus occurs when a market produces less or more than the efficient quantity.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Deadweight loss (DWL) is the reduction in total surplus — consumer surplus plus producer surplus — caused by a market distortion that moves output away from the competitive equilibrium quantity. It appears as a triangle on a supply-and-demand graph between the socially optimal Qe and the actual Q. Taxes, price controls, monopoly pricing, and uncorrected externalities all create DWL. AP FRQs ask students to identify, shade, and compare DWL triangles before and after policy interventions.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs · $100: Additional cost of producing one more unit.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs
questions.explanation: Marginal cost (MC) is the additional cost of producing one more unit: MC = Delta TC / Delta Q. MC curves are U-shaped — they fall initially due to specialization then rise due to diminishing marginal returns. In competitive markets, MC above AVC is the short-run supply curve (firms produce where P = MC). Allocative efficiency requires P = MC. On externality diagrams, marginal social cost (MSC) = MC + external cost per unit.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs · $200: Additional revenue from selling one more unit.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs
questions.explanation: Marginal revenue (MR) is the additional revenue from selling one more unit: MR = Delta TR / Delta Q. For competitive firms, MR = P (demand is perfectly elastic). For monopolists, MR < P and the MR curve lies below the demand curve with twice the slope. All profit-maximizing firms produce where MR = MC. AP FRQs frequently test whether students correctly draw MR for a competitive firm versus a monopolist.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs · $300: Output decision where marginal revenue equals marginal cost.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs
questions.explanation: Profit maximization occurs at the output where MR = MC (provided P >= AVC in the short run; P >= ATC for economic profit). For competitive firms, this means P = MC; for monopolists, they restrict output so MR = MC < P, creating allocative inefficiency. Economic profit = TR - TC = (P - ATC) x Q, shown as a rectangle on a graph. AP FRQs test students' ability to identify the profit-maximizing quantity, price, and profit area for different market structures.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs · $400: Outcome where goods are produced at the lowest possible cost.
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs
questions.explanation: Productive efficiency occurs when a firm produces at the minimum point of its average total cost (ATC) curve, meaning no resources are wasted. In long-run competitive equilibrium, firms produce at minimum ATC (where P = min ATC), achieving both productive and allocative efficiency. Monopolies are productively inefficient because they typically produce at output levels above or below minimum ATC. AP FRQs distinguish productive from allocative efficiency.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs · $500: Per-unit cost found by dividing total cost by output.
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Production + Costs
questions.explanation: Average total cost (ATC) = TC / Q = AVC + AFC. The ATC curve is U-shaped: AFC falls continuously as output rises, while AVC first falls then rises due to diminishing marginal returns. MC intersects ATC at ATC's minimum — the break-even point for competitive firms. Economic profit per unit = P - ATC; loss per unit = ATC - P. AP FRQs ask students to identify ATC graphically and compute profit or loss rectangles.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition · $100: Market with one seller and barriers to entry.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition
questions.explanation: A monopoly is a market with one seller who faces the entire downward-sloping demand curve and sets MR < P. The monopolist maximizes profit where MR = MC, charges price on the demand curve above MR = MC, and produces below the competitive quantity, creating deadweight loss and allocative inefficiency. Barriers to entry (patents, control of key inputs, economies of scale) prevent rivals from entering. AP FRQs compare monopoly and competitive outcomes on the same graph.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition · $200: Declining extra output from additional variable inputs.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition
questions.explanation: Diminishing marginal returns (DMR) occur when adding more variable inputs to a fixed input eventually reduces the marginal product of each additional unit. This is a short-run concept. Graphically, the marginal product curve peaks and falls, causing the MC curve to reach its minimum then rise. DMR is the fundamental reason for the upward-sloping portion of the MC and AVC curves. Long-run economies of scale are a distinct concept unaffected by DMR.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition · $300: Market with many firms selling differentiated products.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition
questions.explanation: Monopolistic competition features many firms selling differentiated products with low barriers to entry, giving each firm some price-setting power (downward-sloping demand curve). Short-run economic profit attracts entry, shifting each firm's demand left until long-run equilibrium at P = ATC (zero economic profit) but P > MC, so allocative inefficiency persists. AP FRQs ask students to draw both short-run profit and long-run zero-profit equilibria for monopolistic competitors.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition · $400: Study of strategic decisions among interdependent actors.
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition
questions.explanation: Game theory analyzes strategic decisions among interdependent actors whose payoffs depend on others' choices. A payoff matrix shows outcomes for each combination of strategies. The dominant strategy (if it exists) is best regardless of what rivals do. Nash equilibrium is the stable outcome where no player can improve by unilaterally changing strategy. In oligopoly, game theory explains why firms in a cartel often defect (prisoners' dilemma) even though cooperation would maximize joint profits.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition · $500: Market with many firms selling identical products.
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Perfect Competition
questions.explanation: Perfect competition has many price-taking firms selling identical products with free entry/exit and perfect information. Each firm faces a perfectly elastic (horizontal) demand curve at the market price, so P = MR = AR. In long-run equilibrium, P = minimum ATC (zero economic profit, productive efficiency) and P = MC (allocative efficiency). AP FRQs ask students to draw short-run profit/loss and long-run adjustment for a perfectly competitive firm and industry.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Monopoly · $100: Government policy limiting monopolies and anti-competitive behavior.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Antitrust policy uses laws (Sherman Act 1890, Clayton Act 1914) and regulatory oversight to prevent monopolies, break up trusts, and prohibit anti-competitive mergers and practices like predatory pricing or exclusive dealing. The goal is to maintain competitive markets that achieve allocative efficiency and protect consumers. AP FRQs may ask how antitrust policy affects market structure, prices, and output compared with an unregulated monopoly.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Monopoly · $200: Charging different prices to different consumers for the same product.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Price discrimination occurs when a seller charges different prices to different buyers for the same product based on willingness to pay, not cost differences. First-degree (perfect) discrimination captures all consumer surplus; third-degree charges different groups different prices (e.g., student vs adult tickets). Price discrimination requires market power, ability to segment markets, and prevention of resale. It increases monopolist profit and can reduce deadweight loss compared with single-price monopoly.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Monopoly · $300: Obstacles that make it difficult for new firms to enter a market.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Barriers to entry prevent or deter new competitors from entering a profitable market, sustaining monopoly or oligopoly power. Types include: economies of scale (natural monopoly), patents and copyrights, control of essential inputs, government licenses, network effects, and high startup costs. Without barriers, economic profit attracts entry until long-run equilibrium with zero profit. AP FRQs use barriers to explain why monopoly and oligopoly profit persists in the long run.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Monopoly · $400: Game in which individual incentives push players toward a worse collective outcome.
questions.explanation: The prisoner's dilemma is a game in which two players each have a dominant strategy to defect (cheat), producing a Nash equilibrium that leaves both worse off than if both had cooperated. In oligopoly, cartel members face this: each firm has an individual incentive to undercut the cartel price, so collusion is unstable even though all would profit from holding the agreed price. AP FRQs use payoff matrices to identify dominant strategies and Nash equilibria.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Monopoly · $500: Market structure in which one seller has substantial control over price.
questions.explanation: A regulated monopoly is a firm with market power whose prices or conduct are controlled by government. For a natural monopoly (declining LRATC), regulators may set price at P = ATC (fair-return pricing, zero economic profit) or P = MC (allocative efficiency but requires a subsidy if MC < ATC). Without regulation, the monopolist maximizes profit where MR = MC, charging a higher price and producing less than is socially optimal.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Oligopoly · $100: Variable cost per unit of output, found by dividing variable cost by quantity.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Average variable cost (AVC) = TVC / Q. The AVC curve is U-shaped; it falls due to specialization then rises due to diminishing returns. The minimum AVC point is the shutdown point: if P < min AVC, the firm loses less by shutting down than by operating. The MC curve intersects AVC at its minimum. AP FRQs ask students to identify whether a firm should shut down or continue operating by comparing price to AVC.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Oligopoly · $200: Long-run cost advantage that occurs when average total cost falls as output increases.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Economies of scale occur when long-run average total cost (LRATC) falls as output increases, giving larger firms a cost advantage. They arise from specialization, spreading fixed costs, and bulk purchasing. When LRATC continuously declines, a single large firm can supply the market more cheaply than many small firms — the natural monopoly case. Beyond minimum efficient scale, diseconomies of scale set in (LRATC rises). AP FRQs use the U-shaped LRATC to identify these regions.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Oligopoly · $300: Lost total surplus caused by inefficient output.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Deadweight loss (DWL) is the reduction in total economic surplus caused by under- or over-production relative to the competitive equilibrium quantity. It appears as a triangle between the demand curve and the supply (MC) curve at quantities away from Qe. Taxes, monopoly pricing, price controls, and uncorrected externalities all generate DWL. The size of DWL depends on the magnitudes of demand and supply elasticities — more elastic curves create larger DWL triangles.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Oligopoly · $400: Outcome where resources go to the uses consumers value most, often where price equals marginal cost.
questions.explanation: Allocative efficiency occurs when output is at the level where P = MC, meaning the marginal benefit to buyers equals the marginal cost to producers and no welfare gains remain unexploited. It is achieved in long-run perfect competition but violated in monopoly (P > MC) and monopolistic competition (P > MC in long run). AP FRQs frequently require students to identify whether a given market structure achieves allocative efficiency and explain why or why not.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Oligopoly · $500: Point where total revenue equals total cost, leaving zero economic profit.
questions.explanation: The break-even point is where P = ATC: the firm covers all costs (explicit and implicit/opportunity) and earns zero economic profit — also called normal profit. For a competitive firm this is the long-run equilibrium. A profit-maximizing firm produces at MR = MC; if P = ATC at that quantity, economic profit is zero. If P > ATC, economic profit is positive; if P < ATC, the firm incurs a loss. AP FRQs ask students to identify the break-even price on a cost-curve graph.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy · $100: Market where one firm can supply output at lower cost than many firms.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy
questions.explanation: A natural monopoly exists when one firm can supply the entire market at lower cost than multiple firms, because LRATC continuously declines over the relevant range of output (large fixed costs, small marginal costs). Examples include water utilities, electricity grids, and railroads. Without regulation, the natural monopolist maximizes profit at MR = MC, setting P > MC. Regulators typically impose fair-return pricing (P = ATC) or marginal-cost pricing (with subsidy).
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy · $200: Per-unit cost curve when all inputs can change and firms can adjust scale.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy
questions.explanation: The LRATC (long-run average total cost) curve shows the minimum average cost achievable at each output level when all inputs — including plant size — can be varied. It is the envelope of all short-run ATC curves. The downward-sloping region shows economies of scale; the upward region shows diseconomies. Minimum efficient scale (MES) is the lowest output at minimum LRATC. Natural monopolies have continuously falling LRATC; competitive industries reach MES at relatively small scale.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy · $300: Market dominated by a few interdependent firms.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy
questions.explanation: An oligopoly is a market dominated by a few large, interdependent firms whose pricing and output decisions depend on rivals' responses. Barriers to entry sustain profit. Models include the prisoner's dilemma, Nash equilibrium, collusion/cartels, and the kinked demand curve. Because firms are interdependent, game theory is essential. AP FRQs test students' ability to use payoff matrices, identify Nash equilibria, and explain why cartels tend to break down (prisoner's dilemma).
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy · $400: Game outcome where no player can improve by changing strategy alone.
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy
questions.explanation: A Nash equilibrium is a combination of strategies from which no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing their own strategy, given what other players are doing. It is not always the best collective outcome — the prisoner's dilemma Nash equilibrium is worse for all players than mutual cooperation. AP FRQs present payoff matrices and ask students to identify each player's dominant strategy and locate the Nash equilibrium, then evaluate whether it is Pareto-efficient.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy · $500: Short-run point where a firm stops producing because price cannot cover average variable cost.
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Firm Strategy
questions.explanation: The shutdown point is where P = min AVC. If P falls below minimum AVC, the firm should shut down immediately (produce Q = 0) because variable costs cannot be covered and losses exceed fixed costs alone. If P >= min AVC, the firm operates in the short run even at a loss, because TR covers at least some fixed costs. In the long run, the firm exits if P < min ATC. AP FRQs test this distinction: short-run shutdown vs long-run exit.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Firms and Market Structures · Oligopoly Strategy · $700: This choice gives a player the best payoff no matter what the other player does.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.topic: Firms and Market Structures · Oligopoly Strategy
questions.explanation: A dominant strategy is one that yields the highest payoff for a player regardless of what any other player does. When all players have dominant strategies, the dominant strategy equilibrium is also a Nash equilibrium. In the prisoner's dilemma, 'defect' (cheat) is each player's dominant strategy, producing a Nash equilibrium that is worse for both than mutual cooperation. AP FRQs give payoff matrices and ask students to identify dominant strategies, locate Nash equilibria, and evaluate efficiency.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Externalities · $100: Cost or benefit affecting third parties.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: An externality is a cost or benefit that spills over to third parties not directly involved in a market transaction, causing the market to produce at the wrong quantity. Negative externalities (e.g., pollution) mean private costs understate social costs (MSC > MPC), so markets overproduce. Positive externalities (e.g., education) mean private benefits understate social benefits (MSB > MPB), so markets underproduce. AP FRQs require drawing both types and identifying the corrective policy.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Externalities · $200: Extra cost to society from one more unit, including private and external costs.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Marginal social cost (MSC) = marginal private cost (MPC) + marginal external cost (MEC). When a negative externality exists (e.g., pollution), MSC lies above the supply curve (MPC). The socially optimal output is where MSC = MSB, which is less than the free-market equilibrium where MPC = MPB. AP FRQs ask students to draw MSC, identify the socially optimal quantity, and show the overproduction and deadweight loss that result from the negative externality.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Externalities · $300: External cost such as pollution.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: A negative externality arises when production or consumption imposes costs on uninvolved third parties (e.g., factory pollution harming nearby residents). Because private producers ignore these external costs, MSC > MPC and the free market overproduces relative to the social optimum. Corrective policies include Pigouvian taxes (raising MPC to MSC), tradable permits (cap-and-trade), and direct regulation. AP FRQs require drawing the externality graph and identifying the overproduction gap.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Externalities · $400: External benefit such as education.
questions.explanation: A positive externality arises when production or consumption benefits uninvolved third parties (e.g., education raises civic participation and productivity for society). Because private buyers ignore these external benefits, MSB > MPB and the free market underproduces relative to the social optimum. Corrective policies include subsidies, government provision, or mandates. AP FRQs ask students to draw the positive externality graph, identify underproduction, and show the corrective subsidy.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Externalities · $500: Government rules designed to reduce pollution, protect resources, or limit negative externalities.
questions.explanation: Environmental regulation uses government rules — emission standards, technology mandates, quantity limits — to reduce negative externalities by forcing producers to internalize external costs. Command-and-control regulation sets output or emissions limits directly. Compared with market-based tools (Pigouvian taxes, tradable permits), direct regulation can be less cost-effective because it doesn't allow the cheapest abatement to occur first. AP FRQs compare regulatory approaches and their efficiency implications.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods · $100: People benefit from a good without paying for it.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods
questions.explanation: The free rider problem occurs when individuals can benefit from a nonexcludable good without paying for it, leading private markets to underprovide or not provide the good at all. Because producers cannot exclude nonpayers, they cannot charge a price — so profit-seeking firms won't supply the good voluntarily. Public goods (like national defense and public fireworks) require government provision funded by taxes to overcome the free rider problem. This is a core AP Micro market failure topic.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods · $200: Resource that is rival but difficult to exclude users from.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods
questions.explanation: A common resource is rival (one person's use reduces availability for others) but nonexcludable (difficult to prevent access). Examples include ocean fisheries, groundwater, and public grazing land. Because no one owns the resource, individuals overuse it, leading to the tragedy of the commons — depletion of the shared resource. Solutions include private property rights, government regulation (quotas), or cooperative management. AP FRQs classify goods using the rival/excludable framework.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods · $300: Situation where one party knows more than another.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods
questions.explanation: Asymmetric information occurs when one party to a transaction knows more than the other, creating market failures. Adverse selection (pre-contractual) arises when the better-informed party uses private information to their advantage (e.g., health insurance — sicker people buy more). Moral hazard (post-contractual) arises when insured parties take greater risks (e.g., reckless driving after insuring). Solutions include signaling, screening, warranties, and mandatory disclosure.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods · $400: Good or service society believes people should consume more of, often supported through subsidies or public provision.
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods
questions.explanation: A merit good is one society believes people should consume more of than they freely choose — often because of positive externalities or information failure. Examples include vaccines, education, and seatbelts. Governments promote merit goods through subsidies, mandates, or direct provision. The concept involves a paternalistic judgment that private preferences undervalue the good's social benefit, distinguishing it from pure public goods (which are nonexcludable and nonrival).
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods · $500: Good that is nonexcludable and nonrival.
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Public Goods
questions.explanation: A pure public good is both nonexcludable (cannot prevent nonpayers from consuming) and nonrival (one person's consumption does not reduce others' availability). National defense and public fireworks are classic examples. Because of the free rider problem, private markets underprovide public goods and government provision funded by taxation is typically required. AP FRQs classify goods using the two-by-two rival/excludable matrix alongside private goods, common resources, and club goods.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure · $100: Situation where markets do not allocate resources efficiently.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure
questions.explanation: Market failure occurs when free markets fail to allocate resources efficiently, producing a situation where total surplus could be increased by a different allocation. The four main sources are externalities, public goods (free rider problem), information asymmetry (adverse selection, moral hazard), and market power (monopoly). Each justifies a different government intervention. AP FRQs require students to identify the type of market failure and explain the appropriate corrective policy.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure · $200: Tax designed to make producers or consumers pay the external cost of an activity.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure
questions.explanation: A Pigouvian tax (named for economist Arthur Pigou) is set equal to the marginal external cost of a negative externality, forcing producers to internalize the spillover cost by raising MPC to MSC. The optimal Pigouvian tax equals the external cost per unit at the socially optimal quantity, reducing output from the free-market level to the social optimum and eliminating deadweight loss. AP FRQs ask students to identify the tax amount on an externality graph and explain how it corrects overproduction.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure · $300: Market-based pollution policy that caps emissions and lets firms buy or sell permits.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure
questions.explanation: Tradable pollution permits (cap-and-trade) set a total emissions cap, allocate permits to firms, and allow trading. Firms that can reduce emissions cheaply sell permits to high-abatement-cost firms, achieving the pollution target at minimum total cost. Unlike a Pigouvian tax, cap-and-trade guarantees a specific quantity of emissions reduction but lets the market determine the permit price. AP FRQs compare tradable permits, Pigouvian taxes, and direct regulation as externality corrections.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure · $400: Market diagram showing a positive externality where social benefit exceeds private benefit.
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure
questions.explanation: The external benefit graph shows a positive externality: MSB (marginal social benefit) lies above the MPB (demand) curve by the external benefit per unit. The free-market equilibrium (where MPB = MPC) underproduces relative to the social optimum (where MSB = MSC). The area between MSB and MPB from Qmarket to Qoptimal represents the deadweight loss from underproduction. A subsidy equal to the external benefit per unit corrects the failure by shifting demand (MPB) up to MSB.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure · $500: Market problem that happens when buyers or sellers lack accurate information for good decisions.
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Market Failure
questions.explanation: Information failure occurs when buyers or sellers lack accurate information needed for efficient decisions, causing market outcomes to diverge from the optimum. George Akerlof's 'market for lemons' model showed that information asymmetry can cause market collapse (e.g., used car markets). Solutions include government mandates for disclosure (food labels, financial reporting), licensing requirements, and consumer protection law. AP Econ treats information failure as a distinct category of market failure alongside externalities and public goods.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency · $100: Extra benefit to society from one more unit, including private and external benefits.
choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency
questions.explanation: Marginal social benefit (MSB) = marginal private benefit (MPB) + marginal external benefit. For goods with positive externalities, MSB > MPB, so markets underprovide — buyers ignore the spillover benefits to third parties. The social optimum occurs where MSB = MSC. On the externality graph, MSB lies above the demand curve (MPB) by the per-unit external benefit. AP FRQs ask students to draw MSB, identify the optimal quantity, and calculate the corrective subsidy.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency · $200: Extra cost paid by producers or consumers for one more unit, excluding external costs.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency
questions.explanation: Marginal private cost (MPC) is the additional cost borne by the producer for one more unit of output, excluding any external costs imposed on third parties. MPC is represented by the supply curve in a competitive market. When a negative externality exists, MSC > MPC by the external cost per unit. The free market equates MPC to MPB, overproducing relative to the social optimum (where MSC = MSB). A Pigouvian tax raises MPC to equal MSC.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency · $300: Overuse of a shared resource.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency
questions.explanation: The tragedy of the commons (Garrett Hardin, 1968) describes how individually rational actors overuse a shared, nonexcludable, rival resource, depleting it for everyone. Each user ignores the cost imposed on others, so aggregate usage exceeds the socially optimal level. Fisheries, groundwater, and congested roads are real-world examples. Solutions include privatization (assign property rights), government-imposed quotas, or cooperative management regimes.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency · $400: Extra benefit received by consumers for one more unit, excluding external benefits.
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency
questions.explanation: Marginal private benefit (MPB) is the additional benefit received by the consumer of one more unit, excluding any external benefits to third parties. MPB is represented by the demand curve. When a positive externality exists, MSB > MPB by the external benefit per unit, causing markets to underproduce. Bringing market output to the social optimum requires a subsidy that raises the effective MPB to the level of MSB.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency · $500: Legal monopoly right that rewards invention by temporarily limiting who can use an innovation.
questions.topic: Market Failure and Government · Equity + Efficiency
questions.explanation: Patent protection grants inventors a temporary monopoly right (20 years in the U.S.) to profit from their innovation, providing the incentive to invest in costly R&D. Without patents, competitors could free-ride on innovations, reducing the private return to R&D below its social value — a positive externality problem. The trade-off is that patents create monopoly pricing and deadweight loss during the patent period. AP Econ treats patents as balancing innovation incentives against static efficiency.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Market Failure and Government · Government Action · $100: Spillover cost or benefit affecting people outside the market transaction.

AP FRQs frequently ask about stagflation (adverse supply shock shifts SRAS left, raising prices and lowering output).

Rightward shifts increase real GDP and price level; leftward shifts cause recession. AP Macro FRQs test all four components and their shifters.

AP FRQs test the relationship between unemployment and the business cycle and Phillips Curve.

When the price level rises, firms' output prices rise faster than input prices, so production becomes more profitable and output expands. SRAS shifts with changes in input costs, productivity, or expectations. An adverse supply shock (leftward SRAS shift) raises prices and reduces output simultaneously — stagflation — a key AP Macro scenario.

On the AD-AS diagram, the AD-SRAS intersection lies to the left of LRAS. The economy self-corrects over time (wages fall, SRAS shifts right), but governments often respond with expansionary fiscal (more G or less T) or monetary policy (buy bonds, lower ff rate) to close the gap faster.

AP Macro FRQs use LRAS as the benchmark for identifying output gaps and evaluating long-run policy effects.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

An adverse supply shock (e.g., oil price spike) shifts SRAS left, raising the price level and reducing real GDP simultaneously (stagflation) — the key distinction from a demand shock. A positive supply shock (e.g., new technology) shifts SRAS right. AP Macro FRQs ask students to draw the AD-AS graph showing the shock and predict changes in price level, real GDP, and unemployment.

Demand-pull inflation results from rightward AD shifts; cost-push inflation from leftward SRAS shifts. Inflation erodes purchasing power, distorts relative prices, redistributes wealth from creditors to debtors, and creates uncertainty. The Fed targets 2% inflation using monetary policy tools. AP Macro FRQs analyze inflation type, cause, and corrective policy.

questions.prompt: Macro Indicators and AD-AS · Unemployment · $400: Output gap where actual output is above full-employment output.

On the AD-AS diagram, the AD-SRAS intersection lies to the right of LRAS. The economy self-corrects as wages rise, shifting SRAS left back to LRAS, but governments may use contractionary fiscal (less G or more T) or monetary policy (sell bonds, raise ff rate) to cool inflation faster. AP FRQs require identifying and closing inflationary gaps.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.prompt: Macro Indicators and AD-AS · Unemployment · $500: Unemployment from frictional and structural causes.
questions.explanation: The natural rate of unemployment (NRU) is the unemployment rate when the economy is at full employment, consisting only of frictional (between-jobs) and structural (skills-mismatch) unemployment — cyclical unemployment is zero. NRU is also called NAIRU (non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment). On the Phillips Curve, NRU is the unemployment rate at which inflation neither accelerates nor decelerates. Policies cannot permanently reduce unemployment below NRU without accelerating inflation.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

questions.explanation: Automatic stabilizers are fiscal mechanisms that automatically change government spending or tax revenues in response to economic conditions without new legislation, dampening business cycle swings. standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

Independence is valued because it reduces the political business cycle — politicians' temptation to loosen policy before elections. The Fed's structure (14-year terms for governors, self-funded) provides institutional independence. AP Macro FRQs may ask students to evaluate tradeoffs between accountability and independence in monetary policy.
standardRefs: economic reasoning with incentives, models, markets, policy, and tradeoffs

Inflation rate = (CPI_t - CPI_{t-1}) / CPI_{t-1} x 100. The CPI may overstate inflation due to substitution bias and quality improvements. AP Macro FRQs use CPI to compute inflation rates, convert nominal to real values, and analyze the cost of living.

Because discretionary policy requires legislation, it suffers from implementation lags (recognition, decision, and effect lags), reducing its timeliness compared with automatic stabilizers or monetary policy.

A positive demand shock (e.g., surge in consumer confidence, fiscal stimulus) shifts AD right, raising both price level and real GDP in the short run (demand-pull inflation if above LRAS). A negative demand shock (e.g., financial crisis, tax hike) shifts AD left, causing recession. Unlike supply shocks, demand shocks move price level and real GDP in the same direction. AP Macro FRQs require drawing and explaining AD shifts.

On the AD-AS graph, leftward SRAS raises the price level and lowers real GDP simultaneously, moving to a new short-run equilibrium with higher prices and an output gap. Stagflation is difficult to address because expansionary policy fights recession but worsens inflation, while contractionary policy fights inflation but deepens recession. The 1970s oil shocks are the classic example.

Expansionary fiscal policy (increase G, cut T) or monetary policy (buy bonds, lower ff rate) shifts AD right to close the recessionary gap. AP FRQs ask students to identify the trough and prescribe appropriate stabilization policy.
